claim_chain: Isolating sEVs from small plasma volumes is challenging
Neither SEC nor DGUC alone provides optimal isolation
SEC-DGUC combination yields higher sEV purity and yield than DGUC-SEC
Small-tube format DGUC reduces time and improves handling
High-purity isolates enable sensitive downstream analyses
Protocol is reproducible and suitable for standardization

typical_pattern: Describe what was tested or compared
Present key findings with supporting details
Acknowledge limitations or caveats where relevant

likely_priorities: Reproducibility and standardization of isolation protocols
Purity and yield quantification of sEV isolates
Comparison with existing methods in the literature
Practical applicability to small clinical sample volumes
likely_attacks: Potential damage to or aggregation of sEVs during ultracentrifugation
Low-level HDL contamination remaining after protocol
Iodixanol interference with certain downstream analyses
Missing lower density sEVs that require longer centrifugation
Variations in plasma samples impacting sEV purity
Need for careful manual handling introducing variability
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The discussion explicitly lists limitations: 'The SEC-DGUC protocol, though efficient, has limitations like potential damage to or aggregation of sEVs and low-level contamination with HDL. The iodixanol reagent introduced in DGUC could potentially interfere with certain downstream analyses, and variations in plasma samples might impact sEV purity. Although the protocol effectively isolates representative sEVs from plasma, some lower density sEVs that require a longer centrifugation time may be missed.' The paper also emphasizes standardization ('It is of particular importance to standardize the sEV isolation protocol') and reproducibility ('highly reproducible'), suggesting these are anticipated reviewer priorities. The comparison with existing methods and validation using FCM and mass spectrometry indicate awareness of the need for rigorous characterization.

phrase_evidence.phrase: The task of isolating sEVs from human plasma becomes particularly challenging when dealing with minimal volumes
To tackle this challenge, our study scrutinized two commonly employed methods
We found that neither SEC nor DGUC, when used individually, provide an optimal solution
Interestingly, our results showed that performing SEC prior to DGUC yields a higher yield of sEVs
This could explain the lower yield of sEVs observed in the DGUC-SEC protocol compared to the SEC-DGUC protocol
More importantly, DGUC with the small-tube format is done using a fixed-angle rotor, which has a shorter sedimentation path length
The advantage of high-purity sEV isolates was demonstrated by FCM and mass spectrometry
suggesting a close representation of sEVs isolated from SEC-DGUC to the original plasma
Therefore, we conclude that the sEVs isolates obtained using the small-volume plasma SEC and DGUC protocol are of sufficient yield and purity
The key to the consistency of the SEC-DGUC protocol lies in three aspects
Collectively, our results clearly demonstrated the quality of the isolated sEVs and the robustness of the SEC-DGUC protocol
This is not to claim that the existing protocols are less reliable than the currently developed protocol
In fact, there is room to further improve the protocol in future efforts
The SEC-DGUC protocol, though efficient, has limitations like potential damage to or aggregation of sEVs
could potentially interfere with certain downstream analyses
variations in plasma samples might impact sEV purity
some lower density sEVs that require a longer centrifugation time may be missed
Isolating high-purity sEVs from small volumes of plasma is essential in fully realizing the potential of using sEVs as biomarkers
It is of particular importance to standardize the sEV isolation protocol so that the data obtained by different labs can be compared and integrated with high confidence
The SEC-DGUC protocol presented here is relatively short, easy to carry out, and isolates sEVs with high purity in a repeatable and reliable manner
It potentially offers a pathway for the standardization of sEV isolation from plasma
500 μL of plasma was loaded on a SEC column and particle fractions (PFs) were collected
Cryo-EM images of PFs obtained from the plasma of five he
Both protocols successfully isolated high-purity sEVs; however, the SEC-DGUC combination yielded higher sEV protein and RNA content
In conclusion, the optimized SEC-DGUC protocol is efficient, highly reproducible, and well suited for isolating high-purity sEVs from small blood volumes

verification_status.notes: All extracted phrases are literal substrings from the input text. No external references, invented numbers, or author names were introduced. The profile is based solely on the provided abstract, discussion, and figure legend excerpts.
